title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder

tex_statement.html: A CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.

tex_proof.raw:
% Claim proof file for row claim_3_2 -- "AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. A
% manager agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the body below.
% The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_2 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_2}
\def\rowtitle{AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_2}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Lem}
        A CDMG  $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.
\end{Lem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
% Proof lifted verbatim from lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex, lines 228-244
% (the \Claude{...} block immediately following the Lem); the outer \Claude{%
% ... }%end Claude wrapper is stripped.
\begin{proof}
($\Leftarrow$)
Suppose $<$ is a topological order of $G$, i.e.\ $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$.
If there were a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v$ ($n \ge 1$),
then $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$, a contradiction.

\medskip\noindent($\Rightarrow$)
Suppose $G$ is acyclic. We construct a topological order by repeatedly selecting a node with no parents in the remaining graph.
Define a sequence $v_1, \dots, v_K$ ($K = |J \cup V|$) inductively: given $v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}$, let $R_i := (J \cup V) \sm \{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}$
and $G_i$ the subgraph of $G$ induced on $R_i$.
Since $G_i$ is acyclic (as an induced subgraph of an acyclic graph) and finite, it has a node $v_i$ with $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$
(otherwise, following parent edges would produce a directed cycle).
Choose any such $v_i$.

This gives a total order $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$.
If $v = v_j \in \Pa^G(w = v_i)$, then when $w$ was selected, $v$ was still in $R_i$, so $v$ would have been a parent of $w$ in $G_i$, contradicting $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$---unless $v$ was already selected, i.e.\ $j < i$. Hence $v < w$.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
($\Leftarrow$) Suppose $<$ is a topological order of $G$, i.e.\ $v \in \Pa^G(w) \implies v < w$. If there were a non-trivial directed walk $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n = v$ ($n \ge 1$), then $v_0 < v_1 < \cdots < v_n = v_0$, a contradiction.

\medskip\noindent($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $G$ is acyclic. We construct a topological order by repeatedly selecting a node with no parents in the remaining graph. Define a sequence $v_1, \dots, v_K$ ($K = |J \cup V|$) inductively: given $v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}$, let $R_i := (J \cup V) \sm \{v_1,\dots,v_{i-1}\}$ and $G_i$ the subgraph of $G$ induced on $R_i$. Since $G_i$ is acyclic (as an induced subgraph of an acyclic graph) and finite, it has a node $v_i$ with $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$ (otherwise, following parent edges would produce a directed cycle). Choose any such $v_i$.

This gives a total order $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_K$. If $v = v_j \in \Pa^G(w = v_i)$, then when $w$ was selected, $v$ was still in $R_i$, so $v$ would have been a parent of $w$ in $G_i$, contradicting $\Pa^{G_i}(v_i) = \emptyset$‚Äîunless $v$ was already selected, i.e.\ $j < i$. Hence $v < w$.

lean.title: AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder
lean.comments:
--
-- A CDMG `G = (J, V, E, L)` is *acyclic* iff it *has* a topological
-- order. In Lean this is the iff `G.IsAcyclic ‚Üî
-- G.HasTopologicalOrder`, using the existential-closure predicate
-- from `TopologicalOrder.lean` (def_3_8) on the right-hand side.
--
-- The (‚áê) direction (topological order ‚áí acyclic) does not need
-- finiteness: a non-trivial directed walk
-- `v = v_0 ‚ü∂ v_1 ‚ü∂ ... ‚ü∂ v_n = v` would force
-- `v_0 < v_1 < ... < v_n = v_0` via `parent_lt` + transitivity,
-- contradicting `irrefl` at `v_0`. The (‚áí) direction (acyclic ‚áí
-- topological order), however, *does* need finiteness ‚Äî the LN
-- proof inducts over `K = |J ‚à™ V|` by repeatedly extracting a
-- parent-free node, which requires `J ‚à™ V` to be finite for
-- termination. We add a `[Finite Œ±]` instance hypothesis on the
-- iff to cover this; see the design-choice block below for the
-- discussion of `Finite Œ±` vs `Fintype Œ±` vs `(G.J ‚à™ G.V).Finite`.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (Lem after
lean.statement:
def 3.8, lines 222 
\begin{claimmark}
\begin{Lem}
        A CDMG  $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is acyclic if and only if it has a topological order.
\end{Lem}
\end{claimmark}
-/
theorem isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder
    [Finite Œ±] (G : CDMG Œ±) :
    G.IsAcyclic ‚Üî G.HasTopologicalOrder := by
  refine ‚ü®?_, ?_‚ü©
  ¬∑ 
    intro hac
    let r‚ÇÄ : Œ± ‚Üí Œ± ‚Üí Prop := fun v w => ‚àÉ œÄ : Walk G v w, œÄ.IsDirected
    have hr‚ÇÄ_refl : ‚àÄ v, r‚ÇÄ v v := fun v => ‚ü®Walk.nil v, by simp‚ü©
    have hr‚ÇÄ_trans : ‚àÄ x y z, r‚ÇÄ x y ‚Üí r‚ÇÄ y z ‚Üí r‚ÇÄ x z := by
      rintro x y z ‚ü®œÄ‚ÇÅ, h‚ÇÅ‚ü© ‚ü®œÄ‚ÇÇ, h‚ÇÇ‚ü©
      exact ‚ü®œÄ‚ÇÅ.append œÄ‚ÇÇ, directedWalk_append œÄ‚ÇÅ œÄ‚ÇÇ h‚ÇÅ h‚ÇÇ‚ü©
    have hr‚ÇÄ_antisymm : ‚àÄ x y, r‚ÇÄ x y ‚Üí r‚ÇÄ y x ‚Üí x = y := by
      rintro x y ‚ü®œÄ‚ÇÅ, h‚ÇÅ‚ü© ‚ü®œÄ‚ÇÇ, h‚ÇÇ‚ü©
      by_contra h_neq
      cases œÄ‚ÇÅ with
      | nil _ => exact h_neq rfl
      | cons s p =>
        cases s with
        | forward h_e =>
          have hx : x ‚àà G :=
            CDMG.mem_iff.mpr (Set.mem_prod.mp (G.E_subset h_e)).1
          have h_cycle_dir : ((Walk.cons (.forward h_e) p).append œÄ‚ÇÇ).IsDirected :=
            directedWalk_append _ _ h‚ÇÅ h‚ÇÇ
          have h_cycle_pos :
              1 ‚â§ ((Walk.cons (.forward h_e) p).append œÄ‚ÇÇ).length := by
            rw [Walk.length_append, Walk.length_cons]; omega
          exact hac x hx ‚ü®_, h_cycle_dir, h_cycle_pos‚ü©
        | backward _ => simp at h‚ÇÅ
        | bidir _ => simp at h‚ÇÅ
    haveI : IsPartialOrder Œ± r‚ÇÄ :=
      { refl := hr‚ÇÄ_refl, trans := hr‚ÇÄ_trans, antisymm := hr‚ÇÄ_antisymm }
    obtain ‚ü®s, hs_lin, hrs‚ü© := extend_partialOrder r‚ÇÄ
    haveI : IsLinearOrder Œ± s := hs_lin
    refine ‚ü®fun v w => s v w ‚àß v ‚âÝ w, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_‚ü©
    ¬∑ 
      intro v _ ‚ü®_, hne‚ü©; exact hne rfl
    ¬∑ 
      intro v _ w hw u _ ‚ü®hsvw, hne_vw‚ü© ‚ü®hswu, _‚ü©
      refine ‚ü®_root_.trans hsvw hswu, ?_‚ü©
      intro h_eq
      subst h_eq
      exact hne_vw (_root_.antisymm hsvw hswu)
    ¬∑ 
      intro v _ w _
      rcases eq_or_ne v w with rfl | hne
      ¬∑ exact Or.inr (Or.inl rfl)
      ¬∑ rcases total_of s v w with h | h
        ¬∑ exact Or.inl ‚ü®h, hne‚ü©
        ¬∑ exact Or.inr (Or.inr ‚ü®h, hne.symm‚ü©)
    ¬∑ 
      intro v w h_pa
      obtain ‚ü®hv, h_edge‚ü© := h_pa
      have hr0 : r‚ÇÄ v w :=
        ‚ü®Walk.cons (.forward h_edge) (Walk.nil w), by simp‚ü©
      have hsvw : s v w := hrs v w hr0
      have hne : v ‚âÝ w := by
        intro h_eq; subst h_eq
        exact hac v hv
          ‚ü®Walk.cons (.forward h_edge) (Walk.nil v), by simp, by simp‚ü©
      exact ‚ü®hsvw, hne‚ü©
  ¬∑ 
    rintro ‚ü®r, hr‚ü© v hv ‚ü®œÄ, h_dir, h_pos‚ü©
    obtain ‚ü®_, _, h_rvv‚ü© :=
      topo_lt_of_directed_walk_pos hr œÄ h_dir h_pos
    exact hr.irrefl v hv h_rvv

lean_explanation:
The theorem `isAcyclic_iff_hasTopologicalOrder` proves the iff `G.IsAcyclic ‚Üî G.HasTopologicalOrder` for a CDMG `G : CDMG Œ±`, assuming `[Finite Œ±]`. The proof is split with `refine ‚ü®?_, ?_‚ü©` into the forward and backward directions.

For the forward direction (acyclic implies topological order), the proof avoids the textbook inductive node-removal argument and instead builds the order via a partial order extension. It defines an auxiliary relation `r‚ÇÄ v w := ‚àÉ œÄ : Walk G v w, œÄ.IsDirected` (the directed reachability preorder). Reflexivity uses `Walk.nil`; transitivity glues walks via `Walk.append` and `directedWalk_append`; antisymmetry uses `by_contra` and `cases` on the walk: the `nil` case contradicts disequality, while a non-trivial `cons` step builds a positive-length directed cycle that contradicts `hac`. The `backward` and `bidir` step cases are dispatched by `simp` since `h‚ÇÅ : IsDirected` rules them out. Once `IsPartialOrder Œ± r‚ÇÄ` is registered as an instance, `extend_partialOrder` (a Mathlib-style order-extension principle, ultimately Szpilrajn) yields a linear refinement `s`. The strict order `fun v w => s v w ‚àß v ‚âÝ w` is then shown to be irreflexive, transitive, total, and parent-respecting (each edge gives `r‚ÇÄ v w`, hence `s v w`, and acyclicity forces `v ‚âÝ w`).

The backward direction is short: from a topological order `r` and a positive-length directed walk, `topo_lt_of_directed_walk_pos` yields `r v v`, contradicting `hr.irrefl`.
design_choices:
The comments motivate two key choices. First, the two directions have asymmetric finiteness needs: the easy `(‚áê)` direction only needs `parent_lt` plus transitivity to derive `v_0 < v_1 < ... < v_0` and `irrefl`, so it is finiteness-free; but the `(‚áí)` direction in the lecture notes inducts over `K = |J ‚à™ V|` by repeatedly extracting a parent-free node, which requires the carrier to be finite for termination. Rather than weakening one side, the iff is stated under a single `[Finite Œ±]` hypothesis covering both.

Second, the comment explicitly flags a `Finite Œ±` vs `Fintype Œ±` vs `(G.J ‚à™ G.V).Finite` trade-off. Using `[Finite Œ±]` (a Prop-valued, non-computable typeclass) keeps the statement clean and matches Mathlib idioms for existence-only arguments like order extension; `Fintype Œ±` would force a `Decidable`-laden constructive version, and `(G.J ‚à™ G.V).Finite` would mean restricting the order to a subset rather than `Œ±`, complicating the predicate `HasTopologicalOrder` whose order lives on all of `Œ±`. The actual proof then sidesteps the inductive node-removal entirely by invoking `extend_partialOrder` on the reachability preorder `r‚ÇÄ`, which is a cleaner use of the finiteness hypothesis than re-running the textbook induction.
